channel.title: AI 书籍精选
channel.description: 由 AI 从多本书中精选的章节内容和智能推荐

item.title: [微信读书] 你越想赶走的念头，越会缠着你
item.description:
你是否也有过这样的体验：深夜失眠时，心里突然冒出一个让自己都吓一跳的念头，然后你拼命摇头想把它甩掉，结果它像胶水一样黏得更紧。你越是在内心疯狂呐喊“不要想了”，那个画面就越清晰，越让你感到羞耻或恐惧。
心理学研究者詹姆斯·彭尼贝克和约书亚·史密斯在《书写的疗愈力量》中给出了一个反直觉的建议：停止对抗。书中写道：“如果你正在被一些禁忌的，或者不能够被接受的想法困扰着，你应该允许自己去想他们，停止一些强迫自己停止思考的举动。”这不是放纵，而是一种深刻的接纳——让那些不能被接受的想法变得可以被接受，恰恰是迈向健康思维的第一步。
这个道理就像沼泽里的挣扎，越是用力，沉得越快。而当你摊开四肢、平静地漂浮时，反而获得了生机。
书里还揭示了一个更令人意外的发现：仅仅是“说出来”这个动作本身，就具备疗愈力。很多人以为在小组谈话或心理咨询中，倾听才是学习的核心，但研究告诉我们一个相反的结论——你对谈话掌控得越多，你认为学到的东西就越多。也就是说，张嘴说的那个人，往往比安静听的那个人获益更大。作者甚至将此上升到了本能的高度：“人有一项基本需要，就是向别人表露自己。”
然而现实中，我们背着太多沉默的重担。那些说不出口的秘密、不愿触碰的创伤，看似被封存，实则在暗处啃噬着我们的身心。书中用大量实证指出：“心怀秘密可能会对我们有潜在危害，而直面个人的想法和感受，却有着显著的短期或者长期的健康益处。”哪怕当时倾诉的代价很大——可能会难堪、会流泪、会暂时更痛——但“说出内心痛苦的秘密能够减少人内心的焦虑和生理应激”。
这背后藏着一个奇妙的转化机制。当我们把一团模糊的痛苦用语言说出来或写下来时，它就从一团混沌的情绪变成了一个有边界、可以审视的“东西”。作者精准地指出：“想要理解一件事情的一个非常有效的方法就是去谈论它，把它转译成语言。”一旦它是基于语言的，我们就能更好地理解这个经历，也许最终能够真正地放下它。
书中提到了一个几乎让人尴尬的临床案例：一位患者长期受哮喘困扰，某天他自己突然意识到，哮喘发作与对父母的某些压抑情绪之间存在着隐秘关联。奇妙的是，当他“意识到父母与哮喘之间的关联后，哮喘就再也没有发作了”。这听起来像是安慰剂效应或巧合，但作者用大量神经科学和心身医学的证据告诉我们，“认罪之后，我们的大脑和身体就会得到放松。一旦理解了自己的心理事件和复发的健康问题之间的关联，身体状况会得到改善。”
基于这些发现，作者提出了一种简单到几乎令人怀疑的技术：表达性书写——每天花15到20分钟，连续三四天，记录一段不愉快的经历。规则只有一条：“在书写中，请以自我反思的方式探索你最深刻的想法和感受。”不是为了写出漂亮的文章，不是为了给别人看，只是给自己一个摊开内心褶皱的机会。因为“书写像绘画一样，是一种自然的人类活动。它的价值之一是帮助我们整合并组织复杂的生活。”
当然，这本书保持了难得的谦逊。它没有宣称书写是万能药，相反，它希望你“成为自己的科学家”，不断在自己的生活中做实验。如果书写对你没帮助，那就去尝试心理治疗、锻炼、冥想，总之——实验、实验、再实验。
或许今天你可以问一问自己：有哪些被我攥得太紧的秘密，其实正在等着被松手变成语言？
（来源：《书写的疗愈力量（原书第3版）》詹姆斯·彭尼贝克、约书亚·史密斯）

item.title: [微信读书] 抑郁的本质，竟是一种美德？
item.description:
在心理治疗的漫长历史中，人们几乎默认了一个前提：只要把问题讲清楚，就能某种程度上获得治愈。于是我们反复挖掘童年创伤，纠正那些被称之为“扭曲”的思维，试图修复功能失调的关系。整个框架就像一个精密的医学模型，为心理治疗提供了文化尊重和经济优势，但也留下了一个被忽视的盲区——我们过度关注患者身上什么是错的，却极少问一句：什么是正确的、什么是坚强的、什么是美好的？
这正是马丁·塞利格曼与塔亚布·拉希德在《积极心理学治疗手册》中试图扭转的局面。他们提出的方法并非否认痛苦，而是换一个起点。书中有一个极其温柔却反直觉的判断：抑郁可能不是一种纯粹的缺陷，而是某种美德的“过度版本”——“一种过度的谦逊（不愿表达自己的需求）、一种过度的善良（以牺牲自我意义为代价来对待他人）、一种过度的洞察力（狭隘的现实观），以及一种过度的意义感（导致过度关注和无法松懈的责任感）”。
这些描述让人停顿几秒。原来那些被诊断为“病”的特质，本可能是品格优势，只是因为走到了极端，才变成牢笼。而积极心理治疗的逻辑由此变得清晰：与其想办法最小化痛苦，不如“评估、鉴别并放大患者的优势，并将这些优势作为治疗的杠杆”。
这个翻转的意义深远。人类大脑天生对消极经历更敏感、反应更强烈，这是进化的遗产。如果治疗只围绕问题旋转，患者学到的往往只是“如何不死板、不无趣、不墨守成规、不抱怨”——一套以“不”字开头的生存技能。而专注于优势，则让患者“学到更自然、更有趣、更有创意、更有感恩之心的具体技能”，这意味着一个人不仅能从疾病中恢复，还能主动建构一种充实、满意并且有意义的生活。
意义这个词，在书中被推到了核心位置。塞利格曼和拉希德写道：“人生的意义在于利用自己的显著优势来归属并服务于比自己更大的事物。”这不是一句轻飘飘的鸡汤，而是整个治疗框架的支柱。积极心理治疗的最终目标，是帮助患者掌握那些与个人息息相关的、可应用的技能，让他们能最大限度发挥自身优势，在朝向内在激励的目标奋斗中，获得真正的治愈与成长。
而这一整套方法对临床医生同样构成提醒。书中提及，累积的共情经验可能导致情感衰竭、人格解体、倦怠乃至同情疲劳。当医生也被卷入只盯着痛苦的漩涡，双方的能量都将被耗尽。相反，“通过培养积极情绪，增强人们的品格优势，帮助他们追求人生的意义，培养积极的人际关系”，才是患者和医者共同走向治愈的关键。
书里有一段话带着一种近乎宣言般的郑重：“初心者，仍为爱智求知，或辅以知识惠施于众，许能动之人寻幸福之生活。积极心理学者虽万念不移此心。”这可能不像典型临床手册的语气，但恰好暴露了这门学科真正的底色——它从一开始就不是只想修正错误，而是想帮助能动之人找到幸福生活的路径。
如果抑郁是美德过度延伸的结果，那么治疗便不是消灭美德，而是帮它找回平衡的尺度。也许，我们都该停下来想一想：你身上那些被当作问题的部分，是否只是某个被遗忘的优势，等待着被重新理解和使用？
（来源：《积极心理学治疗手册》塔亚布·拉希德、马丁·塞利格曼）

item.title: [微信读书] 幸福不是“改正缺点”，而是“做你自己”
item.description:
你有没有过这样的感受：明明日子过得还可以，但就是觉得哪里空落落的？看了很多自我提升的书，学了一堆“应该做的事”，却依然感觉不到真正的满足？也许问题不在于你不够努力，而在于你努力的方向恰恰错过了幸福的核心。
积极心理学之父马丁·塞利格曼在《真实的幸福》中戳破了一个我们深信不疑的幻觉——幸福来自改正所有缺点、修补所有短板。而他说出了一句反直觉的话：
> “我不认为你应该花太多时间去改正自己的弱点；相反，我认为生命最大的成功在于建立及发挥你的优势。”
这话听起来甚至有点“不负责”。我们从小被教育要克服缺点、查漏补缺，怎么到了他这里，缺点反而可以放一放？他的逻辑很清晰：用尽全力去修补弱点，你最多只能变成一个“没有明显缺陷的普通人”。而你真正发光、真正感到充实和满足的时刻，几乎都发生在你运用自己最突出优势的时候。换句话说：
> “幸福感来自于自己的优势与美德，通过自己努力获得的幸福才会有真正的幸福感受。”
这才是“可控的幸福”。不是靠运气等来的好心情，而是在每一天的生活中，有意识地辨别并运用你独有的优势，无论在工作、休闲还是陪伴孩子时。塞利格曼把这种状态称为“美好的生活”——它不是一种遥远的理想，而是一种日常实践：
> “美好的生活来自每一天都应用你的突出优势，有意义的生活还要加上一个条件——将这些优势用于增加知识、力量和美德上。”
读到这儿你可能想问：那我怎么知道自己是不是走对了方向？有一个很简单的检验标准——看你的情绪把思维带到了哪里。塞利格曼发现：
> “冷漠、消极的情绪会激发一种挑剔的思维方式：集中注意去挑毛病，然后宣判出局。相反，积极的情绪会使思维进入有创造性、包容性、建构性、非防御的大道。”
所以当你持续做让自己感到有价值、能发挥所长的事，你的思维会变得越来越开阔，你更愿意帮助别人，更愿意分享，甚至觉得陌生人也可爱起来。而当你陷入消极情绪时，你的世界会急剧缩小，你开始只盯着自己的伤口，变得怀疑和封闭。
书里有一段对比特别锋利——关于我们怎么处理“过去”和“比较”。塞利格曼说：
> “对过往的美好时光不能心存感激和欣赏，对过去的不幸夸大其词、念念不忘，这两种行为是我们得不到平静、满足和满意的罪魁祸首。”
另一面，他还说：
> “幸福不是竞争，真实的幸福来自于提升你的精神层次，而不是与别人相比。”
这两条几乎把现代人精神内耗的两大源头连根拔起：一是对过去耿耿于怀，二是在横向比较中不断焦虑。而解药其实就藏在同一套逻辑里——感恩和宽恕让你与过去和解，发挥优势让你不再需要用别人的坐标来定位自己。
他甚至把这条逻辑铺到了恋爱、育儿、日常社交中。比如处理分手，他说这是一个最后的镜头，会留在记忆里，影响你未来是否愿意再次走进亲密关系。再比如教养孩子，他有一句让很多家长醍醐灌顶的话：
> “我现在终于明白教养孩子绝不仅仅只是修正他的缺点，同时还要发掘他的优势与美德，帮助孩子在社会上找到一个安身立命之所，使他的积极人格特质得以全面发展。”
这不只是育儿智慧，这几乎是重新定义了“什么是真正对一个人好”。
而最让人意外的一点可能是——塞利格曼并不否认痛苦的存在，但他把解释痛苦的方式作为分水岭。他指出：
> “悲观的人常常以致命的方式建构自己所遭受的打击和挫折，他们很自然地认为造成这个挫折或失败的原因是永久的、普遍的，而且全是自己的原因。”
但乐观的人并非盲目，而是把困难看作“暂时的、有主控权的、只在这个情境下才会如此”。这里没有鸡汤，只有一个可操作的认知切换。
说到底，这本书在反复论证一件事：幸福不是一种被赐予的状态，而是一种需要你主动选择、主动培养、主动运用的能力。你必须找出自己的优势，然后每一天，真实地使用它。
那么，你有没有一个一直在“改正”却收效甚微的缺点，其实只是因为——它根本不该是你此刻的主战场？你愿意试着用一天时间，去刻意发挥一个你最突出的优势，看看生活会有什么不同吗？
（来源：《真实的幸福》马丁·塞利格曼）

item.title: [论文] 当注意力开始抛硬币
item.description:
你有没有感觉，现在大模型动不动就宣称能一口气读几十万字，可真的丢给它一堆超长文档去找信息，经常答得牛头不对马嘴。过去大家总觉得是训练没跟上、或者数据没喂够长内容，但最近看到一篇论文，它把矛头指向了几乎所有主流模型都在用的位置编码机制——RoPE，说这事儿可能从数学上就有天花板，不是简单加数据就能解决的。

RoPE这玩意儿听起来玄，其实可以想象成在给每个词贴位置标签时，不是直接写“第几位”，而是让它在一个高维空间里转一个角度。角度大小取决于位置，两个词算注意力的时候，就靠旋转的差值来判断彼此有多远。距离近的高频旋转区分得很清楚，距离远的低频旋转让注意力分数慢慢衰减，天然就有了“越远越不重要”的倾向，这就是 RoPE 被夸的“局部性偏好”。Llama 3、Mistral、Qwen 这些模型都靠它吃饭。

但这篇论文换了个角度看问题：当距离拉得特别长，高频旋转的波动就像收音机调频调出的大量杂音，互相干扰得一塌糊涂。作者把整个 RoPE 分数当成随机变量，用中心极限定理一算，发现分数渐渐逼近一个正态分布——信号（距离带来的衰减均值）被噪声（高频叠加出的方差）慢慢淹没。信号越弱，模型判断“谁离我近”就越靠概率。

顺着这个思路，他们推导出了四种在长上下文下必然会发生的“失灵”模式。

第一种叫位置反转，这是最直观的。本来模型应该给离得近的词打更高分，可一旦上下文变长，这种远近偏好就崩了。论文证明，随着长度增加，一个近处的词和一个远处的词相比，注意力分数谁高谁低，概率会慢慢趋近 0.5，也就是纯粹抛硬币。拿 Llama 3 常用的 RoPE base=500,000 来算，在 128k 上下文下，出现位置反转的几率已经直逼 50%，等于模型完全不知道哪个词离自己更近。

第二种叫位置混叠，这是个数值精度造成的问题。我们用的 BF16 浮点数能表达的分数种类有限，但上下文一长，需要分辨的相对距离数量远超这个上限，于是就会出现两个不同的位置，算出完全一样的注意力分数。好比地图上两个不同的路口，标注了相同的编号，导航肯定要带错路。

接下来两种就和内容有关了。第三种是 token 反转，举个具体例子：你问“养什么宠物”，在近处模型可能正确偏好“猫”这个答案；可同样的问题和选项埋到几万 token 之外，它可能莫名其妙就倒向“狗”了。词的相关性排序在长距离下会颠倒，这让任何依赖位置的信息检索都很不可靠。最后一种是 token 混叠，两个不一样的词在同一个位置，竟然能算出一样的注意力分数，在注意力机制眼里它们就变成同一个东西了，那后面输出什么就可想而知了。

更头疼的是那个“RoPE base”参数。大家为了把窗口撑到百万长度，常常把 base 从默认的一万调到几十万甚至几百万。base 越大，旋转越慢，低频能覆盖的距离更远，位置反转的概率会降下来；但转得太慢，高频的分辨力又不够用了，token 反转和混叠反而会加剧。论文说这是一个没法两全的数学权衡，不存在什么完美的 base 值能同时压住这四类失效。这也解释了为什么猛提上下文窗口的模型，用到后面不是丢信息就是开始自己瞎编。

读完之后我就在想，也许我们对“绝对长度能力”的追求，本身就碰到了一面无形的墙。以后说不定得跳出单纯靠 RoPE 扩窗口的套路，让模型学会动态分配注意力——该仔细看的地方多看看，已经失效的尾巴索性少放精力，总比在后半段一直抛硬币要强。

（论文：RoPE Distinguishes Neither Positions Nor Tokens in Long Contexts, Provably [arXiv:2605.15514]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Challenge of Scaling Context in Transformers

Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly marketed based on their context window size, with claims of processing hundreds of thousands or even millions of tokens. This capability is essential for tasks like analyzing entire codebases, summarizing long documents, or maintaining long-term memory in agents. Central to this capability is the Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE), a mechanism used by models like Llama 3, Mistral, and Qwen to help the attention mechanism understand where tokens are located in a sequence.

Despite these advertised limits, empirical evidence shows that LLMs often struggle with long-context tasks well before they reach their theoretical capacity. Researchers have observed that models frequently lose track of information (the "Lost in the Middle" phenomenon) or fail to retrieve specific facts when hidden deep within a long prompt. This research investigates whether these failures are simply training or engineering issues, or if they represent a fundamental, mathematical limitation of RoPE itself.

Figure 1: An illustration of how positional and token confusion can lead to incorrect model outputs. In the example, the model must distinguish both the identity of the tokens ("dog" vs "cat") and their specific positions to answer a query correctly.

The Mechanics of RoPE

are amplitudes and phases determined by the content of the query and key.

RoPE is designed with a specific frequency schedule. High-frequency components (which rotate quickly) help the model distinguish between positions that are very close to each other. Low-frequency components (which rotate slowly) provide a "locality bias," causing the attention score to generally decay as the distance
𝑚
m increases. This decay ensures that a model naturally prioritizes nearby context, which is often more relevant in natural language.

A Probabilistic View of Positional Encoding

The core theoretical insight of this work is treating the RoPE product as a random variable. When the distance
𝑚
m between tokens is large, the many sinusoidal terms in the sum behave like independent oscillations. According to the Central Limit Theorem, for a sufficiently large context length
𝑀
M, the RoPE product
𝑆
(
𝑚
)
S(m) can be approximated as a normal distribution:

𝑆

)

M
​

M
2
​

(q,k))

Here, the mean
𝜇
𝑀
μ
M
​

is driven by low-frequency terms that provide the locality bias, while the variance
𝜎
𝑀
2
σ
M
2
​

is driven by the high-frequency terms. This probabilistic model allows the authors to prove that as context length grows, the "noise" (variance) from the high-frequency oscillations eventually overwhelms the "signal" (the mean decay).

The Four Failure Modes of RoPE

By analyzing this normal distribution, the paper identifies and proves the existence of four specific failure modes that occur in long contexts. These failures are categorized by whether they affect the identification of positions or the identification of tokens.

1. Position Inversion

Position inversion occurs when a token that is physically further away from the query receives a higher attention score than a token that is closer, purely due to the positional encoding. The authors prove that as context length
𝑀
M increases, the probability of this occurring approaches
0.5
0.5. This means the locality bias—the very feature RoPE is celebrated for—effectively disappears, making the model's preference for near vs. far tokens no better than a coin flip.

2. Position Aliasing

Position aliasing is a numerical failure where two different positions
𝑚
1
m
1
​

and
𝑚
2
m
2
​

produce exactly the same attention score for the same query and key ($ \hat{S}(m_1) = \hat{S}(m_2) $). This happens because of the limited precision of floating-point numbers (like BF16). As context grows, the number of possible positions exceeds the model's ability to represent them uniquely. This leads to "attention invariance," where the model cannot tell which of two positions it is looking at.

3. Token Inversion

While the first two modes focus on position, token inversion affects content. It occurs when the relative ranking of two different keys (e.g., "cat" vs. "dog") flips depending on their distance from the query. For example, a model might correctly prefer "cat" when it's at distance 10, but incorrectly prefer "dog" when both are at distance 50,000, even if the query remains "pet." This makes token relevance inconsistent across long sequences.

4. Token Aliasing

Similar to position aliasing, token aliasing occurs when two different tokens
𝑘
1
k
1
​

and
𝑘
2
k
2
​

at the same position
𝑚
m yield identical attention scores ($ \hat{S}_1(m) = \hat{S}_2(m) $). This renders the tokens indistinguishable to the attention mechanism, potentially leading to hallucinations or retrieval errors.

The Impossible Trade-off of the RoPE Base

To support longer contexts, researchers often increase the "RoPE base"
𝐵
B (typically from 10,000 to 1,000,000 or more). This slows down the rotations, allowing the low-frequency terms to cover a larger range.

However, this paper proves that there is no perfect value for
𝐵
B. Increasing
𝐵
B helps mitigate token inversion and token aliasing (preserving the identity of tokens), but it simultaneously makes position inversion and position aliasing worse (destroying the sense of order). Every attention head has a maximum effective length; beyond this point, it is mathematically impossible for RoPE to distinguish both tokens and positions reliably.

Figure 2: Performance of major LLMs (DeepSeek-V3, Kimi-K2.5, and a 120B model) on a position-identification task. Despite their large size, accuracy drops toward random guessing as context length increases.

Empirical Findings: Theory vs. Practice

The authors verified these theoretical predictions through two sets of experiments.

First, they examined a single attention head in Llama 3.1-8B. They found that for a "pet" query and "cat" key, the attention scores began to oscillate and lose their decay after approximately 50,000 tokens. They also identified thousands of instances of position aliasing where the model could not distinguish between tokens at different distances.

Second, they tested full, multi-layer models on a simple "Indexing Task." In this task, a model is given a Python list of integers and asked to retrieve the value at a specific index. This task requires perfect position identification. The results showed that even top-tier models like DeepSeek-V3 and Llama 3.1—which claim context windows of 128,000 tokens or more—saw their accuracy on this simple task collapse to near-random levels (0.25) at lengths as short as 4,000 to 8,000 tokens.

Conclusion and Future Directions

The significance of this work lies in its shift from engineering to theory. It suggests that the "long-context problem" is not just about having better training data or faster hardware; it is an architectural limitation of how we currently encode position.

If RoPE fundamentally loses its ability to distinguish positions and tokens in long contexts, then simply scaling up existing models will likely yiel

item.title: [论文] 教AI像探险家一样先探路
item.description:
你有没有用过那种帮你订票、操作软件的AI助手？有时候它一上来就按照自己脑子里的“常识”猛冲，结果点错按钮、找不到菜单，然后反复在同一个地方打转，报错好几遍也不知道换个思路。最近看到一篇论文，专门给这个问题起了个名字，叫“过早利用”——就是还没摸清新环境是怎么回事，就急着动手。我觉得这个角度挺有意思的，因为它抓到了现在很多AI智能体实际应用中的一个软肋。

想象一下你出差到一个陌生城市，到了酒店之后你不会立刻开始工作，而是先看看房间布局、插座在哪、网怎么连，甚至试一下浴室花洒怎么开。这些动作跟你要完成的工作目标没啥直接关系，但能让后面做事顺畅很多。论文的核心想法就是这个：给大语言模型驱动的智能体也装上这种“先摸底、再干活”的能力。而且它不是给智能体塞一堆环境说明书，或者提前训练海量场景，而是让智能体自己在线探索，把看到的东西总结成一段接地气的知识，再带着这段知识去执行任务。

那怎么让智能体学会探索呢？这里面有个很聪明的设计——他们定义了一种叫“探索检查点覆盖率”的指标，英文简称ECC。简单来说，就是给每个环境预先埋一些检查点，比如“到达厨房”“找到水壶”“成功打开冰箱门”。一条探索轨迹能撞上多少检查点，就给它打多少分。这些检查点是从环境引擎内部状态提取的，所以评价是客观的、可验证的。这样一来，即使没有任务目标，也能定量地知道这只AI探路探得够不够广。论文里把这个过程形式化成了两阶段范式，叫“Explore-then-Act”，先探索后行动。第一阶段，智能体没有具体任务，只是在预算步数内到处逛，最后把逛到的地形、可操作的东西、动作的后果写成一个自然语言的知识摘要。第二阶段才给它真实任务，并且让它在做决策时参考这份摘要，这样就能避免被自己脑子里的刻板印象带偏。

训练的时候也有讲究。如果只用任务完成与否来奖励智能体，它会变得越来越窄——只记住那条能得分的固定路径，一旦环境稍微变动就歇菜。所以他们用了交替式训练：一部分回合只奖励探索覆盖率，另一部分回合才奖励任务完成。通过这种方式，智能体学会在“到处看看”和“瞄准目标”之间平衡。用的一种叫GRPO的强化学习算法，效果很明显。他们在一些文字冒险游戏类环境里做了实验，采用新方法后，任务成功率从大约四成多一下子蹿到了八成以上，而且反复失败、原地转圈的情况大大减少。更重要的是，智能体发现了一些它原先根本不知道的东西，比如某些意料之外的物体或动作后果，这些发现后来直接帮助了任务执行。

我觉得这个工作有个地方挺有现实启发：它把“探索”当成了一种独立的能力来训练，而不是寄希望于智能体在解决任务时顺便学会。这跟人类的学习过程很像——我们小时候东摸西看，攒下大量没直接用途的经验，长大后才在各种目标里用上。但也有显而易见的局限。先探索再行动，额外耗掉的时间和交互步数不可忽略，如果环境极度危险或者交互成本很高，这种纯探索可能就不划算。另外，探索得来的知识摘要是一次性的，如果环境在探索和行动之间发生了动态变化，比如有人把水壶收走了，智能体可能又会栽跟头。未来也许能让探索变成持续更新的过程，或者只在智能体“感到不确定”时才触发探索，而不是每次都重新摸一遍。

总的来说，这篇论文提供了一个很实用的框架，让AI不再靠死记硬背去适应新环境，而是真正去收集一手信息。读的时候我在想，如果将来的机器人能用上这种策略，被派到地震废墟里先去自己探一圈，回来报告哪里有人、哪条路能走，那该多靠谱。

（论文：Look Before You Leap: Autonomous Exploration for LLM Agents [arXiv:2605.16143]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Challenge of Premature Exploitation in LLM Agents

Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly used to perform complex, multi-step tasks in digital and physical environments. While these agents demonstrate impressive reasoning capabilities, they often struggle when deployed in unfamiliar settings. A primary cause for this failure is a phenomenon identified as "premature exploitation." Instead of systematically exploring a new environment to understand its layout, tools, and constraints, agents often rely on their pre-trained internal priors to act immediately.

Figure 1: Comparison between premature exploitation, where agents fail due to repeated loops and action mismatches, and the proposed autonomous exploration strategy that incentives discovery and a decoupled "Explore-then-Act" paradigm.

When an agent acts on incomplete information, it frequently enters cycles of repetitive, failing actions or attempts to use objects in ways the environment does not allow. Current research has attempted to solve this by providing agents with environment-specific manuals or pre-training them on vast amounts of task-specific data. However, these "offline" solutions are difficult to scale to real-world scenarios where environments are diverse and dynamic. The paper "Look Before You Leap" proposes a shift in focus: endowing agents with the intrinsic capability to perform autonomous, goal-free exploration to acquire grounded knowledge "online."

Formalizing Autonomous Exploration

To address the limitations of existing agents, the authors first formalize autonomous exploration as a distinct capability, separate from goal-directed task execution. In a standard interaction, an agent generates an action
𝑎
𝑡
a
t
​

based on its history
𝐻
𝑡
H

​

and a specific goal
𝑔
g. Success is typically measured by a binary task completion reward
𝑅
(
𝜏

𝑔

R(τ,g).

In contrast, autonomous environment exploration is proactive and information-seeking. It occurs without an assigned goal. The agent interacts with the environment for a fixed interaction budget
𝑁
N, producing an exploration trajectory
𝜏
𝑒
𝑥
𝑝

​

. The purpose of this trajectory is to map the environment's transition dynamics, identify interactable objects, and understand the semantics of different actions. This process results in a grounded knowledge summary
𝐾
K, which serves as a bridge between the agent's general knowledge and the specific reality of the current environment.

Quantifying Discovery with Exploration Checkpoint Coverage (ECC)

A significant hurdle in training agents to explore is the lack of a verifiable metric for "exploration quality." If there is no specific task goal, how can we objectively measure if an agent has explored well? The paper introduces Exploration Checkpoint Coverage (ECC) to solve this.

For any given environment, the authors define a finite set of exploration checkpoints

Locations: Reaching specific rooms or areas.
Objects: Discovering key items required for interaction.
Affordances: Successfully performing a valid action that changes the environment state.

The ECC for an exploration trajectory

Figure 2: An illustration of an agent's trajectory through an environment. The ECC score quantifies the breadth of discovery across locations, objects, and affordances.

The Explore-then-Act (E-t-A) Paradigm

Traditional agent architectures attempt to explore and solve a task simultaneously. This paper proposes decoupling these phases into the "Explore-then-Act" (E-t-A) paradigm. This is a two-stage process:

Exploration Phase: The agent is deployed into the environment without a goal. Guided by an exploration policy
𝜋
𝐸

𝑁

𝐾

Acting Phase: The agent is given a specific task goal
𝑔
g. It then uses an acting policy
𝜋
𝐴

to solve the task. Critically, this policy is conditioned not just on the current history and goal, but also on the knowledge
𝐾

𝜋

By separating these phases, the agent can resolve discrepancies between its internal priors and the environment's actual state before attempting the task, leading to more informed and successful execution.

Training Exploration-Capable Agents

The research demonstrates that standard reinforcement learning (RL) focused solely on task completion actually harms exploration. Agents trained only to maximize task rewards become "narrow," learning to repeat specific paths to the goal while losing the ability to handle deviations.

To counter this, the authors utilize Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and introduce an exploration-aware training strategy. For exploration-focused training steps, the reward is defined by the ECC score:

𝑅
𝐸

The training process uses an Interleaved Schedule, alternating between exploration-focused rollouts and task-focused rollouts. In the exploration rollouts, the agent is rewarded for discovering new checkpoints. In the task rollouts, it is rewarded for successful completion. This dual-objective training ensures the agent learns how to navigate and understand new environments while maintaining its ability to execute specific goals.

Figure 3: Comparison of success rates for different RL configurations. Agents trained with interleaved exploration and task rewards consistently outperform those trained on tasks alone, particularly in the Explore-then-Act setting.

Analysis of Behavioral Changes

The experiments, conducted on environments like ALFWorld, SciWorld, and TextCraft, revealed several key findings regarding agent behavior. When comparing agents trained with task-only RL versus those trained with interleaved exploration RL, the following shifts were observed:

Reduction in Loops: Task-only agents often get stuck in "repeated action loops," performing the same failing action multiple times. Exploration-aware agents reduce these loops significantly (e.g., from 16.0% down to 7.7% in some evaluations).
Information-Seeking Actions: Exploration-trained agents perform more actions specifically designed to gather information, such as "look" or "examine," rather than blindly trying to manipulate objects.
Error Recovery: These agents are more likely to attempt a different strategy after a failure, demonstrating a more grounded understanding of the environment's possibilities.

The study also evaluated how the exploration budget
𝑁
N affects performance. It found that while more exploration generally leads to higher ECC and better task performance, there is a threshold. If the budget is too low, the agent might only gather noisy or incomplete information, which can actually confuse the acting phase. However, with a sufficient budget, the E-t-A paradigm provides a consistent performance gain over direct execution.

Figure 4: (a) Exploration Checkpoint Coverage increases with the step budget, with interleaved RL showing much higher efficiency. (b) The performance gain fro

item.title: [论文] 单步训练，视频模型高效对齐
item.description:
你有没有觉得，现在 AI 生成的视频越来越流畅、细节也越来越好了？这背后其实靠的是超大规模的视频扩散模型。但这些模型在海量数据上学到的只是“平均”视觉分布，未必能真正贴合我们的审美——比如画面美感、对文字描述的执行力，还有物理世界的合理性。为了让模型更懂人类，大家开始用强化学习（特别是 GRPO）来做“对齐”。

但问题来了：视频模型的扩散过程有几十个去噪步骤，传统 GRPO 要把梯度从头传到尾，计算量大得离谱。一个 140 亿参数的模型，一次实验可能要花几百个 GPU·天。有人想偷懒，只随机抽几个步骤来训练，但这样训练不稳定，质量也掉得厉害。最近一个叫 Flash-GRPO 的工作找到了一种巧妙的办法，用单步训练就达到了以前全流程训练的水平，计算量直接砍掉了大半。

他们解决了两件事。第一件是“优势估计”里的噪音。在 GRPO 里，对于同一个文字提示，模型会生成好几个不同视频，然后算出每个样本的“优势”——其实就是这个样本比组里平均好多少。以往的做法是在不同去噪步骤上各自算奖励，相当于给组里每个样本发了一张难易程度完全不同的试卷。这样一来，分数差既可能是因为模型表现好，也可能是因为碰巧抽到了容易的步骤。Flash-GRPO 用一种叫 Iso-Temporal Grouping 的策略，让同一个提示下的所有样本共享同一个随机的时间步，也就是大家考同一张卷子。对比的时候，剩下的差异就纯纯是模型策略带来的了，学习信号干净了很多。

第二件是梯度大小不平衡。扩散模型里，不同时间步产生的梯度幅度天生就差别巨大，有的大得离谱，有的小得可怜。如果不加处理，那些“大梯度”步骤就会主宰参数更新，训练很容易崩溃。他们从数学推导里找到了一个时间相关的缩放因子 λ(t)，然后发明了 Temporal Gradient Rectification ——说白了就是梯度除以这个因子，强行把所有时间步的贡献拉平。这样一来，每一个提示在训练中都能均衡地提供信息，奖励曲线不再上蹿下跳，而是稳稳地往上走。

实验在 Wan2.1 模型上跑过，1.3B 和 14B 参数都试了，用 VBench 和人类偏好评分模型 HPSv3 来评估。结果挺直观：只用单步训练的 Flash-GRPO，不仅在效率上甩开全流程训练好几个身位，视频质量也跟它持平甚至更好一些。原来动辄几百 GPU·天的活，现在用一杯咖啡的功夫就能跑完。我觉得这种方式很务实，把不必要的计算冗余剔除了，又不伤性能。

当然也有一些可以继续琢磨的地方。比如单步训练虽然快，但会不会在某些特别需要精细多步调优的任务上吃亏？而且整个对齐效果还挺依赖奖励模型的准确性，如果奖励模型本身有偏见，结果也会跑偏。未来也许可以试着动态选择关键时间步，或者把奖励模型也一起迭代优化。总之，这个思路让视频模型的对齐从“烧钱”变成了“省钱”，挺值得跟进的。

论文：Flash-GRPO: Efficient Alignment for Video Diffusion via One-Step Policy Optimization [arXiv:2605.15980]

📄 论文原文概述
The development of large-scale video diffusion models has significantly advanced the quality and temporal consistency of AI-generated video. While these models are trained on vast datasets to learn general visual distributions, they often require "alignment" to better reflect human preferences, such as aesthetic beauty, strict adherence to text prompts, and physical realism. A common approach to this alignment is Reinforcement Learning (RL), specifically Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).

However, applying RL to video models presents a massive computational challenge. Standard techniques require backpropagating gradients through the entire denoising process, which involves many sequential steps. For a model with 14 billion parameters, this process can take hundreds of GPU days for a single experiment. While some researchers have tried to speed this up by only training on a few steps of the process, these "subsampling" methods often lead to unstable training and lower quality. Flash-GRPO addresses these issues by introducing a single-step training framework that maintains the performance of full-trajectory training while drastically reducing the computational cost.

Figure 1: The Flash-GRPO framework utilizes Iso-Temporal Grouping and Temporal Gradient Rectification to achieve stable and efficient alignment in a single-step training paradigm.

The Challenge of Video Diffusion Alignment

In the context of diffusion models, the generative process is a sequence of denoising steps. Aligning these models involves optimizing the policy—represented by the model parameters
𝜃
θ—to maximize a reward signal derived from human preference models. GRPO is a popular choice for this because it eliminates the need for a separate "critic" model by calculating advantages within a group of samples. For a given prompt
𝑐
c, the model generates
𝐺
G different outputs. The advantage for each sample is then calculated relative to the group:

𝐴
𝑖
=
𝑟
𝑖
−
mean
(
𝑟
)
std
(
𝑟
)
A
i
​

=
std(r)
r
i
​

−mean(r)
​


where
𝑟
𝑖
r
i
​

is the reward for the
𝑖
i-th sample. In video generation, where each sample is a complex sequence of frames, computing these rewards and the associated gradients across all timesteps
𝑡
t is extremely expensive. Naive attempts to optimize only a single, randomly sampled timestep per iteration often fail because the rewards become highly volatile and the gradients inconsistent across different noise levels.

Iso-Temporal Grouping: Reducing Advantage Noise

One primary reason why single-step training is unstable is "timestep-confounded advantage estimation." In a typical training batch, different prompts are sampled at different timesteps
𝑡

𝐺

Some timesteps are inherently "harder" for the model than others. For example, the very first steps of denoising (high noise) have a different impact on the final output quality than the final refinement steps. If the group baseline mixes these levels, the calculated advantage
𝐴

Flash-GRPO solves this through Iso-Temporal Grouping (ITG). In this approach, for each prompt

𝐺

Temporal Gradient Rectification: Balancing Optimization

The second challenge addressed by Flash-GRPO is "gradient scale heterogeneity." Even with stable advantage estimates, the magnitude of the gradients produced at different timesteps
𝑡

By setting the effective scaling factor to unity across all timesteps, TGR ensures that every prompt in a training batch contributes equally to the update, leading to much smoother training and monotonic reward growth.

Experimental Performance and Benchmarks

The effectiveness of Flash-GRPO was tested using the Wan2.1 video diffusion model across different sizes, including the 1.3B and 14B parameter versions. The model was evaluated on VBench, a comprehensive benchmark for video quality, and against reward models like HPSv3 (Human Preference Score).

Figure 2: Qualitative comparison between the vanilla Wan2.1 model and Flash-GRPO. The aligned model shows improved motion coherence, better aesthetic details, and superior prompt adherence.

The results showed that Flash-GRPO significantly outperformed existing efficient methods. In a comparison with Flow-GRPO-Fast (a naive subsampling method), Flash-GRPO reached a higher performance ceiling and avoided the catastrophic reward drops that plagued the baseline.

Specifically, on the VBench benchmark:

Aesthetic Quality: Flash-GRPO achieved a score of 66.43, higher than the full-trajectory Flow-GRPO (65.79).
Subject Consistency: It recorded a score of 98.70, showing that subjects remain stable throughout the video.
Efficiency: Flash-GRPO achieved these results in roughly 350 GPU hours, whereas previous methods often required thousands of hours to reach similar levels of alignment.

Figure 3: Training reward curves showing Flash-GRPO (blue) achieving stable, monotonic growth compared to the high variance and instability of naive subsampling methods (red).

Stability and Scaling to 14B Parameters

One of the most significant achievements of this work is its stability when scaling to very large models. Aligning a 14-billion-parameter model is notoriously difficult because small instabilities in the gradient can quickly lead to model collapse.

In tests with the Wan2.1-14B model, Flash-GRPO maintained a stable gradient norm and consistent reward improvement. In contrast, the standard full-trajectory approach was not only 6 times slower but also showed signs of instability and performance plateaus. By decoupling the optimization from the specific discretization scale of the sampler, Flash-GRPO allows for reliable alignment of the largest available open-source video models.

Figure 4: Flash-GRPO maintains steady improvement even at the 14B parameter scale, outperforming full-trajectory methods that struggle with instability and high computational costs.

Conclusion

Flash-GRPO provides a principled solution to the efficiency-quality trade-off in video mode

item.title: [论文] 教机器人先弄懂物理再干活
item.description:
你有没有想过，现在的机器人学东西的方式其实挺笨的。大部分模型就是看海量的机器人操作视频，然后模仿动作。这就像一个人只背了10000道菜谱，但只要换个厨房、换个锅，他就不知道该怎么下手了。换一个摄像机角度，或者桌上多了个没见过的透明杯子，模型可能就不动了。

PhysBrain 1.0 这个工作想换一个思路。他们的想法很朴素：与其让机器人死记硬背动作，不如先让它搞懂物理世界是怎么回事。东西会怎么滚、手伸过去能不能够到、碰到东西的时候手感会怎样——这些常识，人类靠日常生活中的第一人称视角积累了很多。论文里的Ego4D数据集就全是这种人类第一视角视频，有人做饭、有人修东西、有人整理房间。把这里面关于物理交互的信息提炼出来，再教给机器人，可能会让它更“明白”自己在干什么。

我觉得他们做数据的方式还挺聪明的。面对一段人做事的视频，他们不是简单地说“这人在切菜”，而是用多个大模型合作，给视频做一套结构化的物理标注。分成三类：场景里有什么东西（材质、形状、状态），这些东西在空间里怎么变化（靠近、接触、翻转），以及动作本身有什么特点（快慢、力的方向）。然后把这些信息变成问答对来训练模型，比如“那个袋子是透明的还是编织的”、“现在离手最近的硬质物体是什么”。

更有意思的是深度信息的加入。视频是二维的，但机器人干活是在三维空间里，必须知道距离。他们用深度估计模型给画面里的物体配上了米级标注，然后生成类似“木板离镜头大概47厘米”这样的问答。这听起来很简单，但它逼着模型把像素和真实的物理尺度联系起来。以后机器人要控制机械臂伸过去，脑子里就有一个三维的场景，而不只是一张平板图。

有了这些物理常识，再让模型去学机器人具体怎么动，就容易多了。但他们也发现一个常见问题：你要是直接用机器人数据去微调，模型很容易忘掉之前学的通用视觉和语言能力。于是搞了个双通路结构，一边是冻结的“物理常识通路”，保持对世界的理解不变；另一边是专门学动作的“具身通路”，在预测动作的时候始终去参考那个冻住的通路。这样动作学习就不会把更基础的理解给冲掉。

效果怎么样呢？他们把 PhysBrain 1.0 放在几个机器人操作的模拟环境里测。比较突出的是在 SIMPLER 这个场景里，基于 BridgeData 数据微调后，成功率从基线模型的 27.7% 提升到了 49.2%，涨了 21.5 个点。在 CALVIN 这种需要长序列任务的环境里也涨了 13.4%。同时在一些通用的视觉语言基准上，分数基本没掉，说明双通路结构确实护住了基础能力。

不过这个工作也有它的边界。论文里对接触状态的判断还比较粗，像“抓得紧不紧”“有没有打滑”这种更细粒度的物理状态，目前还没涉及。而且从物理常识到精确动作之间，如何更好地生成那些需要关节力矩信息的低层控制指令，也还有得研究。但这至少是个挺清晰的尝试——让“理解”走在“动作”前面，说不定能帮机器人少背点菜谱，多学点本质的东西。

（论文：PhysBrain 1.0 Technical Report [arXiv:2605.15298]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Philosophy of Physical Commonsense in Embodied AI

The current landscape of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models is largely defined by a paradigm of scaling: training models on increasingly massive datasets of robot trajectories to fit action policies. While this approach has enabled robots to perform specific tasks, it often lacks a fundamental understanding of physical regularities. A model trained purely on imitation may struggle when faced with a new camera viewpoint, a different scene layout, or an object with unfamiliar material properties.

PhysBrain 1.0 introduces a shift in this methodology, operating under the principle of "Understanding first, action next." Rather than relying solely on robot-specific data—which is expensive, platform-dependent, and inherently limited—this research proposes acquiring general physical commonsense from large-scale human egocentric video before adapting the model for robot control. By leveraging the vast amount of human interaction data available in first-person videos, the model can learn about contact mechanics, object reachability, and spatial constraints in a way that is decoupled from a specific robot's hardware.

Figure 1: Performance summary of PhysBrain 1.0 across various Vision-Language-Action (VLA) simulation benchmarks and multimodal Vision-Language Model (VLM) tasks.

Bridging the Gap Between Human Observation and Robot Action

The transition from watching a human interact with the world to a robot performing the same task is non-trivial. Human videos provide rich semantic and physical information, but they do not contain the motor commands (proprioception or joint torques) required for robot control. The challenge lies in systematically transforming raw human video into structured, actionable physical supervision.

PhysBrain 1.0 addresses this by developing a data engine that extracts latent physical factors from human video. These factors include object arrangements, spatial evolution, and action feasibility. This structured information is then used to train a base Vision-Language Model (VLM) on physically grounded question-answering (QA) tasks. This ensures that the model develops an internal representation of the physical world—understanding how objects move and interact—before it is ever asked to predict a robot's specific end-effector trajectory.

The PhysBrain 1.0 Data Engine: Converting Video to Physics

To move beyond generic video captioning, the PhysBrain 1.0 data engine implements a staged process for data construction. The process begins with raw egocentric video clips from sources like Ego4D and BuildAI, which are filtered for visual quality and camera stability. The engine then uses a multi-model pool (including models like GPT-5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro) to generate structured scene meta-information in JSON format.

Figure 2: The PhysBrain 1.0 data engine architecture, illustrating the transformation of human egocentric video clips into structured scene meta-information and subsequently into physically grounded QA pairs.

The structured meta-information is organized into three primary categories:

Scene Elements: This records static or slowly changing aspects of the environment. It identifies manipulated objects and surrounding items, noting their geometry, material cues, and physical states (e.g., whether a bag is translucent or a tool is rigid).
Spatial Dynamics: This field tracks the initial layout of the scene and how the relationships between the actor and objects evolve. It captures the physics of approach, contact, and reorientation.
Action Execution: This provides a brief task intent alongside a detailed description of the execution, emphasizing the velocity profile and contact physics involved in the interaction.
Structured Knowledge and Depth-Aware Spatial Reasoning

One of the significant hurdles for VLMs in embodied tasks is 3D spatial reasoning. Standard videos are 2D projections of a 3D world, which can obscure the absolute distances and scales necessary for precise manipulation. To ground the model in real-world scale, PhysBrain 1.0 incorporates depth-aware spatial augmentation.

Using advanced depth estimation models, the system associates point-wise depth estimates with objects identified in the scene. This data supports the generation of depth-specific QA pairs, such as:

Relative Depth: "Which object is closer to the camera, the wooden board or the washbasin?"
Metric Distance: Estimating the absolute distance (e.g.,
47
 cm
47 cm) of an object from the observer.

By training on these metrics, the model learns to associate visual features with physical dimensions, a critical requirement for controlling a robot's end-effector in 3D space. The training also enforces a structured "thought process" for embodied reasoning, where the model must first perceive the environment and objects, then perform spatial planning, and finally describe the action execution.

Dual-Pathway Architecture for Capability Preservation

Once the base model has acquired physical commonsense, it must be adapted for robot control. A common problem in this stage is "catastrophic forgetting," where the model's specialized training on robot trajectories erodes its general vision-language capabilities and language sensitivity.

PhysBrain 1.0 introduces a dual-pathway architecture to mitigate this. The system consists of:

Frozen General Pathway: This branch is initialized from the physically informed base VLM and remains frozen. It processes visual and language inputs to provide a stable, high-level semantic reference.
Trainable Embodied Pathway: This branch learns the specific action generation for the robot. It is optimized on robot demonstrations but conditions its predictions on features from the frozen general pathway via asymmetric layer-wise fusion.

This design allows the model to leverage its pre-learned physical and semantic priors without modifying them, ensuring that the robot's "reasoning" remains grounded in the broad knowledge acquired during the video-training phase.

Action Decoding and Language Alignment

To generate smooth and precise robot movements, PhysBrain 1.0 treats action prediction as a trajectory generation problem. Instead of predicting a single discrete action, the model uses a flow-matching objective to decode continuous actions from the hidden states of language-conditioned action queries.

The trajectory

To prevent this, an action-conditioned language alignment objective is introduced. It optimizes the log-likelihood ratio between a language-conditioned policy and a vision-only policy:

Experimental Results: Simulation and Real-World Robots

The effectiveness of PhysBrain 1.0 was validated across a suite of benchmarks, including multimodal VLM tasks, robot simulations, and real-world manipulation.

In multimodal benchmarks, PhysBrain 8B demonstrated improvements in physically grounded reasoning, scoring higher on the PhysBench and RealWorldQA datasets compared to the base Qwen3-VL models. This

item.title: [论文] 给世界模型装上两套眼睛
item.description:
跟你说个挺有意思的架构设计，来自一篇最近的世界模型论文。简单说，他们想让AI从无标签视频里自己学会“什么叫动作”，而且生成的画面还不能糊掉。

先说说为什么这个事值得关注。现在做机器人、自动驾驶的团队，都希望有一套“世界模型”——相当于给AI装个内部模拟器，让它能想象出做完某个动作后环境会变成什么样。但训练这种模型通常需要每一帧都配上精确的动作标签，比如“电机扭矩2.3牛米”或“方向盘左转15度”，这些数据又贵又难搞。另一边，网上有海量的无标签视频，人干活、机器人操作，源源不断。所以这几年很多人研究“潜在动作模型”，试图直接从视频帧之间推演出一个抽象的“动作”，用它来驱动预测。

问题就出在这个推演过程里。如果你要求AI把动作学得很抽象、很精炼，生成出来的下一帧画面就容易丢失细节，变得模糊或者不真实；反过来，如果你让它把画面每个肌理都复现得清清楚楚，那它学出来的“动作”就会跟场景的外观纠缠在一起——比如它会误以为“变亮了”是动作，或者把“背景里有个红球”当成运动指令，而不是关注物体本身的运动规律。这就是所谓的“潜在动作权衡”。

DiLA这套方法想了一个直截了当的解法：干脆把场景的“结构”（什么东西在哪儿、怎么动）和“内容”（颜色、纹理、光线这些东西）拆成两条独立的通路来处理，还设计了一个互相制约的机制，让抽象动作的学习和外观分离互相促进。

你可以这样理解：整个模型像一个内部车间，进料是连续的视频帧，先全部压缩到一个共享的潜空间里（用预训练的DINOv2编码，后面再接一个正则化自编码器解码回画面，这样主训练不用在像素层算来算去）。然后车间分两条流水线。

第一条叫“结构通路”，专门抓几何形状和运动。它把压缩后的视觉tokens进一步提炼成结构嵌入，再让逆向动力学模型（IDM）只读这些嵌入的时序差异。注意，这里关键点是“时序差异”——如果画面里某样东西一动不动，它的结构差异就是零，IDM几乎完全忽略它。这等于是用了一个极自然的信息瓶颈，逼着模型只去关注那些随时间变化的东西，也就是真正的运动。然后这个IDM吐出一串潜在动作向量，交给前向动力学模型（FDM）。FDM是个时空Transformer，但它不做凭空生成，而是预测一个“残差”：当前结构要往哪里变。最终下一个结构就等于当前结构加上这个残差。这种方式让动作信息被压缩得非常精炼。

第二条叫“内容通路”，任务正好相反：专门记住外表细节，比如桌子的木纹、手臂的颜色、背景里的杂物。因为现实世界里常常有遮挡——比如机械臂伸过去挡住了一个盒子——模型需要记住盒子之前长什么样子。这里他们用了一种叫Mamba的选择性状态空间模型来做时序记忆。Mamba擅长处理长序列，记忆保留也稳定，每次读入当前内容嵌入，再结合之前积累的隐状态，输出一个带有整体记忆的内容表征。这就相当于给模型建立了一个关于场景外观的“持续信念”。

两条流水线的产出最后汇合到一个融合解码器里，用的是空间注意力Transformer。核心操作是：以预测出来的结构作为查询（Query），去内容记忆和第一帧原始视觉嵌入里交叉关注。这里特意把第一帧的原始嵌入也加进来，是为了把那些在压缩过程中损失掉的高频细节给补回去，让最终生成的画面更锐利。

这样整个流程的好处就很明显了：动作学得抽象，画面还不糊。因为结构通路只关心运动，内容通路只关心外观，两条线各司其职，谁也不会干扰谁。而且这种分离不是硬切的，而是通过运动信息瓶颈这个约束自然诱导出来的，训练时模型自己就会往这条路上走。

论文里肯定甩了一堆实验数据，比如在哪个机器人视频数据集上预测的FVD（视频质量的衡量指标）降了多少，但我还没来得及细抠那些数字。单从方法论上看，我觉得比较巧妙的一点是把“外观与运动分离”这个老问题，用一种双通道、差异驱动的架构重新具象化了。未来可能的局限，我猜脱离预训练DINOv2编码的话，它对视觉特征的理解可能会打折扣；Mamba虽然高效，但在极端超长序列下的稳定性还得再看。总体来说，这是一种把表征学习、记忆网络和世界模型揉在一起的干净设计，挺值得顺着读一读的。

（论文：DiLA: Disentangled Latent Action World Models [arXiv:2605.15725]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Evolution of World Models and Latent Action Learning

In the pursuit of autonomous agents capable of navigating and interacting with the real world, researchers have increasingly turned to world models. These models function as internal simulators, allowing an agent to predict the consequences of its actions and plan accordingly. Traditionally, training such models requires large datasets where every video frame is paired with an explicit action label—for instance, the specific torque applied to a robot's joint or the direction a vehicle is steered. However, action-labeled data is expensive and difficult to collect at scale. In contrast, unlabeled video data of humans and robots performing tasks is abundant.

To bridge this gap, the field has seen the rise of Latent Action Models (LAMs). These systems aim to learn from unlabeled video by inferring "latent actions"—abstract representations of motion—directly from sequences of frames. A standard LAM consists of an Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM) that deduces what action occurred between two frames, and a Forward Dynamics Model (FDM) that predicts the next state given a current state and a latent action. While promising, LAMs face a significant hurdle known as the "LAM Trade-off."

The LAM Trade-off refers to the inherent conflict between making latent actions abstract and maintaining high-quality video generation. If a model is forced to be very abstract (using techniques like Vector Quantization), the generated video often loses fine details and looks unrealistic. Conversely, if the model is allowed to capture every detail to produce high-fidelity video, the "actions" it learns often become "entangled" with the visual appearance of the scene, capturing irrelevant information like the color of an object or lighting changes rather than the underlying physics of the movement.

Disentangled Latent Action (DiLA) Framework

DiLA (Disentangled Latent Action world model) is designed to resolve this trade-off by explicitly separating the "content" of a scene from its "structure." This approach is built on the hypothesis that disentanglement and action learning can be mutually beneficial. In this framework, the predictive bottleneck required to learn abstract actions actually helps force the model to separate static visual details from dynamic spatial layouts.

The architecture operates entirely within a latent space, utilizing a frozen DINOv2 encoder for feature extraction and a Regularized Autoencoder (RAE) decoder for final visualization. By working in the latent space, the model avoids the computational burden of pixel-level reconstruction during its core training phases. The system processes video through two distinct, specialized pathways: the Structure Pathway and the Content Pathway.

The Structure Pathway and Dynamics Modeling

The Structure Pathway is responsible for capturing the geometry and motion of the scene. It compresses visual tokens into structure embeddings

By tasking the FDM with predicting the next structural state based on these inferred actions, the model is incentivized to distill only the most essential movement-related information into the latent action space.

The Content Pathway and Memory

While the structure pathway focuses on motion, the Content Pathway is dedicated to preserving visual appearance, such as textures, colors, and background details. Because real-world environments are often only partially observable (objects can be hidden behind others), the model needs a way to remember what it has seen previously.

To achieve this, DiLA uses the Mamba architecture, a selective state space model known for its efficient handling of long sequences and stable memory retention. The content encoder produces embeddings

This memory allows the model to maintain a consistent "belief" about the environment. For instance, if a robot arm moves in front of a box, the content pathway remembers what the box looked like so it can reconstruct it accurately once the arm moves away.

Integrating Structure and Content for Generation

Experimental Results and Capabilities

The researchers evaluated DiLA across several dimensions, including its ability to transfer actions, the quality of its generated videos, and the structure of its learned latent space.

Action Transfer Across Embodiments

One of the most significant findings is DiLA's ability to perform cross-embodiment action transfer. Because the latent actions are abstract and decoupled from visual content, an action learned from a human video (e.g., "pick up a cup") can be applied to a robot arm in a different environment. The model demonstrated semantic transfer across diverse objects and ev

item.title: [论文] 省掉2000GB存储，视频生成加速4倍
item.description:
想象一下，你想在AI生成的视频里自由移动视角，就像玩游戏一样按一下键盘，画面就跟着实时变化。现在的视频生成模型大多是整段处理的，延迟太高，根本做不到交互。于是大家转向自回归路线：一帧一帧地生成，每一帧都参考之前的历史。但即使这样，每一帧通常还要跑十几个去噪步骤，离真正的实时（一帧一步）还差得远。最近读到的这篇论文就在解决这个问题——怎样让自回归视频模型既快又稳，而且训练成本不要爆炸。

模型提速的常规操作是“蒸馏”：找一个又慢又精的老师模型，教一个小而快的学生。在自回归视频场景里，这个学生得是“因果”的，只能看过去的帧来生成下一帧，不能偷看未来。整个训练过程通常分三步：先用多步训练一个基础模型，然后初始化一个少步学生，最后用学生自己的生成历史做微调，防止错误越滚越大。最大的坑卡在第二步初始化上，以前的方法要么因为架构不匹配导致画面模糊，要么直接拿多步模型当一步用造成质量崩塌。后来有人提出了 Causal Forcing，用一个因果老师来教学生，解决了不匹配的问题，但它有一个很“重”的代价：老师得提前把每一段视频从纯噪声到清晰画面的完整48步去噪轨迹都算出来并存下来。处理大规模数据集时，这些轨迹要占将近2000 GiB 的存储空间，而且光是跑这些轨迹就耗费了上万个GPU小时。你想，存这么多中间状态、反复读写，成本高到几乎没法用在更大的模型上。

这篇论文叫 Causal Forcing++ ，它的核心巧思是换了一种教法，叫“因果一致性蒸馏（Causal CD）”。我跟你聊聊它是怎么省掉那2000G轨迹的。

可以这样想：传统方法相当于老师把解一道题的全过程一步步写下来，让学生背熟。一致性蒸馏则是，老师每次只示范“往前走一小步”，然后让学生自己领悟：不管在去噪路径的哪个位置，只要沿着同一个方向，最终预测出来的干净画面应该是一样的。具体来说，训练时先拿一个纯噪声的输入，老师用一步把它变成稍微干净一点的状态；然后让学生从这两个状态（原噪声和稍干净的状态）都预测出最终的清晰帧，再让这两个预测结果尽量接近。因为这两个状态其实在同一条去噪轨迹上，它们指向的终点本来就该一致。学生通过这种方式学会在一步之内直接从任意噪声跳到清晰画面，而且整个过程是“在线”的——每次训练只需要老师算一步，不用提前生成和存储整个轨迹；数据用的就是真实视频片段，简单直接。

这个改动带来的好处直接看数字：存储需求从近2000 GiB 降到 0；初始化阶段的训练时间从 11600 A800 GPU小时降到约 2900 小时，差不多四倍加速。而且视频质量跟原来那套又存又算的方法相当，完全没有因为省钱就变差。这也就意味着，我们可以用同样的预算去训练更大的模型、喂更多的数据，这对未来做可交互的生成式世界模型是个挺关键的基础。

论文里还讨论了一个有意思的细节：不同的蒸馏损失函数会带来生成风格上的差异。一致性损失倾向于“模式搜寻”，生成的画面比较锐利，但多样性可能稍微弱一点；另一种分数蒸馏损失则更偏“模式覆盖”，能生成更多样的内容，但有时会偏模糊。他们做了一些权衡实验，最后在视频任务上觉得一致性蒸馏性价比最高。这给后续工作留了一个思考：如果想要更好的多样性，也许可以在训练后期加入一些模式覆盖的机制。

我读下来的感受是，这个工作很聪明地把一个“离线批处理”式的蒸馏过程变成了“在线学习”，解了一大存储和计算上的死结。可能离真正能实时交互的AI视频生成还有一段路（比如一步生成的质量还可以再打磨，历史信息的利用还需要更细致），但至少它把规模化训练的瓶颈打通了。以后如果出现能让你边玩边生成的沙盒游戏，背后很可能就有这类方法的影子。

论文：Causal Forcing++: Scalable Few-Step Autoregressive Diffusion Distillation for Real-Time Interactive [arXiv:2605.15141]

📄 论文原文概述
Real-Time Interactive Video Generation

The demand for interactive video generation has grown as researchers strive to build "world models" that can respond to user inputs, such as camera movements or character actions, in real-time. Traditional video generation models often process entire clips at once, which introduces high latency and prevents immediate feedback. To solve this, researchers have turned to autoregressive (AR) diffusion models. These models generate video segments sequentially, using previous frames as context for the next.

However, achieving true real-time interaction remains difficult. Most current methods operate in a "chunk-wise" fashion, generating several frames (e.g., 4 or 8) at once, often requiring multiple sampling steps per chunk. While this is faster than generating a whole video, it still creates a noticeable lag. The goal is to move toward "frame-wise" generation—producing one frame at a time—using only a single sampling step. Causal Forcing++ addresses the technical hurdles of this aggressive efficiency target by refining how these models are trained and distilled.

Figure 1: Comparison between previous methods like Self Forcing (SF) and Causal Forcing (CF) against the proposed Causal Forcing++ (CF++). CF++ reduces initialization time and storage costs while improving latency and maintaining video quality.

The Challenge of Autoregressive Distillation

The process of creating a fast video model usually involves "distillation," where a complex, slow "teacher" model trains a smaller, faster "student" model. In the context of autoregressive video, this distillation must be "causal," meaning the model only looks at the past to generate the future.

The standard pipeline for this process consists of three stages:

Multi-step AR Training: Training a base model to generate videos autoregressively using many steps (e.g., 48 steps).
Few-step Student Initialization: Taking that multi-step model and teaching it to generate the same content in just a few steps (e.g., 1 or 2).
Self-Rollout Refinement: Fine-tuning the student model using its own generated history to prevent errors from compounding over time.

A major bottleneck occurs at Stage 2. If the student model is initialized poorly, it cannot recover during Stage 3. Previous methods encountered three main issues:

Architectural Mismatch: Some methods distill from a "bidirectional" teacher that sees the whole video. When the "causal" student (which only sees the past) tries to mimic this teacher, it produces blurry results because it lacks the "future" information the teacher used.
Approximation Errors: Simply using a multi-step AR model as a 1-step generator leads to immediate quality collapse because the model wasn't designed to bridge the entire noise-to-image gap in one jump.
Scalability Bottlenecks: The original Causal Forcing method solved the mismatch by using a causal teacher, but it required pre-calculating and storing thousands of gigabytes of "trajectories"—the step-by-step path the model takes to create an image. This made it extremely expensive and difficult to scale to larger datasets.
Causal Consistency Distillation (Causal CD)

The core contribution of Causal Forcing++ is a new initialization strategy called Causal Consistency Distillation (Causal CD). This method replaces the expensive "offline" trajectory generation with an efficient "online" process.

In standard diffusion models, we move from a noisy state
𝑥
𝑡

is a target model (often a moving average of the student). This approach is "online" because it only requires one teacher step during each training iteration, directly using ground-truth video data.

Efficiency and Scalability

By moving to an online consistency objective, Causal Forcing++ eliminates the need for massive auxiliary storage. In previous implementations, storing the ODE trajectories for a large dataset required nearly 2,000 GiB of space. Causal Forcing++ reduces this extra storage requirement to zero.

Furthermore, the training time is significantly reduced. Because the model doesn't have to wait for the teacher to generate full 48-step trajectories for every sample, the initialization stage is roughly four times faster. In experimental setups, this reduced the computation time from 11,600 A800 GPU hours to approximately 2,900 hours. This efficiency makes it feasible to train much larger models on more diverse datasets.

Figure 2: Statistical breakdown of the resource savings provided by Causal CD initialization compared to the previous Causal ODE approach.

Mode-Seeking vs. Mode-Covering: Handling Exposure Bias

One of the nuanced findings of the paper is the comparison between Causal CD and Causal Score Distillation (also known as Causal DMD). In the world of generative models, there is a distinction between "mode-seeking" and "mode-covering" behavior.

Mode-seeking models (like DMD) try to find the absolute "best" or most likely version of an image. While this produces very sharp individual frames, it is dangerous in autoregressive sequences. This is due to "exposure bias"—small errors that occur because the model is generating based on its own previous (imperfect) outputs rather than perfect ground-truth data.

Figure 3: Conceptual illustration of why mode-seeking (Causal DMD) is more sensitive to shifts in distribution (errors) than mode-covering (Causal CD). A small shift in the history causes the mode-seeking model to fall into a "poor quality" region, leading to rapid video degradation.

Because mode-seeking models have a very "sharp" and narrow distribution, a tiny drift in the generated history can push the next frame into a low-probability, poor-quality region. This leads to videos that look good for a few frames but quickly collapse into noise or static images. In contrast, Causal CD is "mode-covering," meaning it maintains a broader distribution that is more robust to the small drifts and errors inherent in self-rollout.

Experimental Results and Real-Time Performance

The researchers evaluated Causal Forcing++ using the Wan2.1-1.3B model at a resolution of
480
×
832
480×832. They measured performance using VBench (a comprehensive video quality benchmark) and VisionReward (which measures how well the video follows text instructions).

The results showed that even with only 2 sampling steps per frame, Causal Forcing++ outperformed previous 4-step chunk-wise models.

Latency: The "first-frame latency" (the time a user waits after a command) was reduced by 50%, from 0.60 seconds to 0.27 seconds.
Throughput: The model achieved higher frames-per-second (FPS), enabling smoother playback during interaction.
Quality: The VBench Total score reached 84.14, exceeding the previous state-of-

item.title: [论文] 让模型学会用单词画图推理
item.description:
你还记得之前那些多模态模型吗？看图说话、识别物体这些都挺溜的，但一到需要一步步推理的视觉任务，比如解几何题、在两张相似图片里找茬，它们就容易卡壳。现在主流的解法分成三派：一种是让模型真的把中间步骤的图像给生成出来，算得太慢了；另一种是让模型当指挥官，去调用外部工具画线、裁剪，但来回折腾工具描述和调用，等待时间巨长；还有一派干脆在模型的“脑海”里（特征空间）默默推理，快是快了，但像个黑箱，没法解释。我一直觉得，能不能有个中间路线，既快又能让人看懂它怎么想的？最近看到这篇论文，它就搞了个有点意思的框架，叫ATLAS。

核心想法其实特别简单：既然模型已经能把文字当成一个一个的词（token）来说，能不能把那些“视觉操作”也变成几个新词，直接塞进它的词典里？比如需要画个箭头表示力的方向，那模型就直接在它的推理台词里说一句“<|Arrow|>”，然后用这个词的效果去驱动画面上的变化，接着再继续文字分析。这就等于让模型在说话的同时，随手就能在脑海里给图片加点注释、画几条辅助线，而不需要真的调出画图软件来干活。

他们给这个新词典加了五个“功能词”：<|Manip|>负责对图像做增强，比如拉近看细节、降个噪；<|Shape|>用来标记特定区域；<|Line|>加几何线条，解数学题时特别好用；<|Arrow|>表示方向、运动或流程；<|Text|>直接在画面上贴文字标签。这些词跟普通文本词一样，被模型在自回归生成时一个个采样出来，所以原来的训练流程和推理架构完全不用改。

有了这五个词，还不够，得让模型学会在恰当的时候“说出”它们。训练分两步走：第一步，用监督学习给模型打基础。他们收集了一个叫ATLAS-178K的数据集，里面13.8万条是原本用代码描述视觉操作的样例，他们把代码解析成那五个功能词，再用一个很强的大模型帮忙把推理过程改写成自然、连贯的文字，这样模型就能看到“遇到什么问题，第几句时该说哪个功能词”的正确示范。另外还混入了4万条纯感知数据，免得模型太专注玩这些新词，把认图的老本行给丢了。第二步，用强化学习去优化最终答案的正确率。奖励函数设计得挺细致：答对给分，而且必须是答对的同时还至少用了一次功能词才给额外奖励，如果狂刷功能词蹭奖励，或者输出得又臭又长，会有惩罚。这样就能逼着模型把功能词用在刀刃上。

可是训练中又冒出一个问题。你想，在一条推理链里，普通文字词可能有上百个，功能词顶多一两个。当用强化学习给整段话打分回传梯度时，那稀稀拉拉的功能词就像在吵闹的教室后排说悄悄话，学习信号全被前面大段大段的文字给淹没了。他们管这个叫“梯度稀释”。为了解决它，他们做了一个挺机灵的小改进：在标准的组策略优化（GRPO）基础上，给功能词额外加了一个词级别的辅助损失，相当于直接在这些关键位置给个高清的、特写镜头式的梯度信号。这个方法叫LA-GRPO，能让模型更扎实地学会在什么时候用什么功能词。

效果怎么样？他们用ATLAS训练了一个20亿参数的模型，然后在好几个数学和视觉推理的测试集上跟其他方法比。结果确实是又快又好：准确率超过了用同样基础模型但走agentic路线的方案，甚至比一些7B、8B的大模型还高，而推理延迟只有agentic方法的几分之一。毕竟不需要真的去调外部软件，一切都在模型自己的代币流里搞定。

我觉得这个思路挺有潜力的，它把“做事”变成了“说话”，让模型可以用统一的语言逻辑去操控视觉元素。不过也有明显的局限，现在能用的操作就这五类，碰到更复杂的需求，比如需要抠图、旋转，可能就不够用了。而且训练数据是依赖另一个大模型去润色推理链的，数据质量受制于人。未来要是能把功能词扩展成更丰富的“视觉动作库”，或者让模型自己学会动态组合这些词来完成新任务，那可就更有意思了。

论文：ATLAS: Agentic or Latent Visual Reasoning? One Word is Enough for Both [arXiv:2605.15198]

📄 论文原文概述
The Landscape of Interleaved Visual Reasoning

Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have transitioned from simple image captioning to complex problem-solving. However, tasks requiring multi-step visual reasoning—such as solving a geometry problem or identifying subtle differences in similar images—often demand more than a single pass of "look and answer." This has led to the development of interleaved visual reasoning, where a model generates, perceives, and updates intermediate visual states to reach a final conclusion.

Currently, this field is divided into three primary paradigms, each with significant trade-offs. Unified models attempt to generate actual pixels for intermediate steps, which is computationally expensive and difficult to train. Agentic reasoning models act as high-level controllers that call external tools or write code to manipulate images; while powerful, this approach is slow and creates high inference latency due to verbose tool-call descriptions and external execution overhead. Finally, latent reasoning methods perform intermediate steps within hidden vector spaces. While faster, these often rely on restricted task-specific supervision and can break the standard parallel training pipelines used for modern autoregressive models.

Figure 1: Comparison of existing visual reasoning paradigms against the proposed ATLAS framework. ATLAS integrates visual operations directly into the token stream, maintaining efficiency and compatibility with standard training.

The ATLAS framework proposes a middle ground. Instead of generating pixels or long code blocks, it introduces a compact set of "functional tokens" directly into the model's vocabulary. This allows the model to treat visual operations (like zooming, drawing, or labeling) as discrete words in its internal reasoning chain. By doing so, ATLAS achieves the interpretability of agentic methods with the speed and scalability of latent reasoning.

The ATLAS Framework: One Word for Every Action

The core innovation of ATLAS is the expansion of the standard VLM vocabulary to include five specific functional tokens. These tokens act as internal commands that represent complex visual operations without requiring external software or pixel-level image reconstruction during the reasoning process.

The expanded vocabulary is defined as
𝑉

​

, where the functional tokens are:

<|Manip|>: Used for general image enhancement, such as denoising, cropping, or zooming in on a specific detail.
<|Shape|>: Used to mark or highlight specific regions, aiding in spatial reasoning or area-based tasks.
<|Line|>: Adds geometric cues or structural lines, particularly useful for mathematical construction.
<|Arrow|>: Indicates motion, force, flow, or directional guidance.
<|Text|>: Adds symbolic labels or numerical values to the visual field of reasoning.

Because these tokens are part of the standard autoregressive sequence, the model learns to "invoke" them when necessary. For example, when solving a physics problem, the model might generate a sequence of text followed by <|Arrow|> to indicate a force vector, and then continue its textual explanation. This design maintains strict compatibility with existing autoregressive training and inference pipelines.

Figure 2: The ATLAS framework architecture. Functional tokens are embedded within the standard vocabulary, allowing the model to sample visual operations just like natural language tokens.

A Two-Stage Training Recipe

To teach the model how and when to use these functional tokens, the researchers employed a two-stage training approach: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) followed by Reinforcement Learning (RL).

Stage 1: Supervised Fine-Tuning with ATLAS-178K

The researchers first curated a dataset named ATLAS-178K. They started with 138K examples derived from existing image-construction datasets. These examples originally used code to describe visual operations. The researchers parsed this code, mapped the operations to the five functional tokens, and then used a high-performance model (Gemini-2.5-Pro) to polish the reasoning trajectories into natural language. This stage provides the model with the basic capability to integrate functional tokens into a logical reasoning flow. Additionally, 40K examples of pure perception data were included to ensure the model did not lose its foundational visual understanding.

Stage 2: Outcome-Driven Reinforcement Learning

After SFT, the model undergoes RL using Group-based Reinforcement Policy Optimization (GRPO). The goal here is to optimize for the final answer's correctness. Since the model must learn to use functional tokens meaningfully—rather than just spamming them to get a reward—the researchers developed a composite reward function

Solving Gradient Dilution with LA-GRPO

A significant challenge identified during training was "gradient dilution." In a typical reasoning sequence, there might be hundreds of standard text tokens but only one or two functional tokens. When applying sequence-level rewards in GRPO, the learning signal for these sparse functional tokens gets overshadowed by the sheer volume of ordinary text.

To address this, the authors introduced Latent-Anchored GRPO (LA-GRPO). This mechanism adds a token-level auxiliary loss specifically for the functional tokens. It reuses the group advantage

Figure 3: Illustration of LA-GRPO. By adding a specific token-level surrogate loss for functional tokens, the framework overcomes the gradient dilution problem that occurs when these tokens are sparse compared to text.

Quantitative Results and Efficiency

The researchers evaluated ATLAS against several benchmarks, including BLINK (visual perception) and WeMath (mathematical reasoning). The results showed that ATLAS, particularly the version trained with LA-GRPO, consistently outperformed the base model (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) and other specialized visual reasoning models.

On the BLINK benchmark, the base model achieved 22.8% average accuracy, while ATLAS with LA-GRPO reached 51.3%. This performance was competitive with much larger closed-source models like GPT-4o. Notably, the ablation studies showed that the LA-GRPO mechanism was essential for this success; without it, the model's use of functional tokens was less stable, leading to lower performance on complex tasks like multi-view reasoning and spatial relations.

Beyond accuracy, ATLAS demonstrated superior efficiency compared to agentic methods. Because it uses single tokens instead of verbose code or tool descriptions, it significantly reduces the sequence length and inference time.

item.title: [论文] 让AI回到过去预测未来
item.description:
你有没有想过，怎么判断一个AI是不是真的能像人一样，根据新闻来调整自己对未来的判断？现在大多数评测都是在固定题目上做测试，一看就会，但真实世界里信息是一点点放出来的，你得不断修正自己的想法。最近看到一篇论文，他们搞了一个挺巧妙的方法——让AI“穿越”回过去，用某一天之前的旧报纸，让它预测当时还没发生的事，然后看它预测得准不准。

这个项目叫FutureSim，本质上是一个按时间顺序回放新闻的模拟沙盒。他们从Common Crawl News（一个互联网新闻存档）里挑出了大量有确切日期的文章，然后把AI放进去，模拟日期一天天往前走。AI每天只能看到“当天”及之前的所有新闻报道，不能用未来的信息。它的任务是对一系列真实发生过的事件（比如某国大选结果、某个体育赛事冠军）做出概率预测。等模拟日期推进到事件发生的日子，系统就会根据真实结果打分。

这套方法好就好在，它既不是纯静态的题库，也不需要真的让AI去实时世界里等几个月看结果。因为用的是历史上的真实事件，结果都是已知的，所以可以反复跑、反复比较，成本低又可复现。而且它能直接测出AI“信念更新”的能力——当新的消息出来，它是死守原来的猜测，还是会合理调整概率？

具体来说，AI在模拟里可以使用几个核心工具。一个叫submit_forecast，允许它针对某个问题给出各个可能结果的概率分布（比如“候选人A当选：60%，候选人B：40%”），并且可以随时更新。另一个是next_day，相当于让它睡一觉，醒来就是新的一天，新闻库会解锁当天发表的文章。最重要的是搜索工具search_news，它只能检索到当前模拟日期为止的新闻。这就严格保证了AI没法“偷看答案”。另外，因为整个过程可能长达数周甚至数月，AI需要自己管理记忆——有的代理一轮模拟下来会读超过1200万个token，执行上千次操作，所以必须自己学会总结和记录关键信息，不能只靠一长串对话历史。

评测指标上，除了最简单的“预测结果对不对”（Top-1准确率），他们更看重Brier Skill Score（BSS）。这个指标的精髓在于：不仅看你猜没猜对，还看你是不是“自信过头”。如果你拍胸脯说某件事99%会发生，结果没发生，BSS会狠狠扣分；反过来，如果你说“大概六成把握”，对了就加分，但不会加太多。BSS=1是完美预测，0相当于瞎蒙或者直接弃权，负分说明你比乱猜还要糟糕。

他们用这套框架测试了GPT 5.5、DeepSeek V4 Pro、Claude Opus 4.6等几个前沿模型，题目是330个在2026年初揭晓的真实世界事件，这些事件的结局都在模型训练数据截止日期之后，所以模型没法靠记忆作弊。结果GPT 5.5表现最好，Top-1准确率25%，而且BSS是正的。很多开源模型一开始跑出负的BSS，说明它们对错误的预测特别自信。25%的准确率听起来不高，但要知道，这些题目通常不是二选一，而是有很多可能结果，随机猜中的概率可能只有个位数，所以25%其实已经表现出了不错的预测能力。

我觉得这个工作的有意思之处在于，它把“预测未来”这个看似只能在线下慢慢等的事情，变成了一个可批量操作的实验。以前想评估一个AI的长期预测能力，要么得让它真的去参与预测市场，耗时长还不可控；要么就用一些简化过的游戏环境，跟真实世界的复杂度差得远。FutureSim用新闻存档作为信息源，既保留了真实世界信息流的“嘈杂”和“渐进而非一次性给出”的特点，又能精确控制时间线，让每次测试的条件一致。

当然它也有局限。新闻文章只是信息的一部分，现实里人们还会通过社交媒体、私人交流、专家解读等来更新信念，而这些渠道没有被包含进去。另外，AI的搜索策略和记忆压缩能力对结果影响很大，有时候模型预测不准可能不是推理能力不行，而是没搜到关键文章或者记漏了。未来如果加入更多类型的数据源，或者延长模拟的时间跨度，可能会更接近真实场景。

总之，FutureSim相当于给AI们出了一场“历史开卷考”：试卷是过去的世界，答案早就有了，但考生必须在不偷看明天报纸的前提下，每天用当天的信息去猜。这种测试方式，或许能帮我们更好地理解，模型到底是在真正“推理”未来，还是只是在复述训练数据里的模式。

（论文：FutureSim: Replaying World Events to Evaluate Adaptive Agents [arXiv:2605.15188]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Challenge of Evaluating Adaptive AI Agents

Evaluating the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) and autonomous agents often relies on static benchmarks or synthetic simulations. While these methods provide useful metrics for specific tasks, they frequently fail to capture an agent's ability to adapt in dynamic, open-ended environments that reflect the complexity of the real world. Traditional benchmarks often serve as proxies for reasoning rather than direct measures of how an agent handles the temporal evolution of information.

The researchers behind FutureSim argue that true adaptation should be measured by an agent's capacity to process and integrate new information over long horizons. Existing environments like games or human-designed simulations often lack the fidelity required to mimic societal shifts. Meanwhile, live deployments on prediction markets or real-world tasks are expensive, slow, and nearly impossible to reproduce under controlled conditions. FutureSim addresses these gaps by using real-world event timestamps to create a replayable, grounded simulation. By providing agents with access to a chronological archive of news, researchers can observe how agents update their beliefs and adjust their strategies as "the future" unfolds in the simulation.

Grounded Simulations through Chronological Replay

The core principle of FutureSim is the "chronological replay" of world events. Instead of a static dataset, the environment functions as a time-gated sandbox. It utilizes a massive, reliably dated archive of news articles—specifically from the Common Crawl News (CCNews) dataset—to ensure that agents only see information available up to a specific simulated date.

This approach offers two primary advantages:

Belief Alignment and Updating: It tests whether an agent's initial priors are consistent with the world and how effectively the agent updates those priors when presented with new evidence. This mirrors the behavior of human expert forecasters who must constantly refine their predictions.
Groundedness in Reality: Because the simulation is based on events that have already occurred in the real world, the agent's actions (like submitting a forecast) do not change the underlying environment. This avoids the "counterfactual problem" found in interactive simulations where an agent's move might fundamentally alter the future, making evaluation against real history impossible.

The simulation operates on a daily cadence. At each time step, the agent can search the available corpus, manage its internal memory, and submit or update forecasts. As the simulation advances to the next day, the environment state updates, new articles become available, and any questions reaching their resolution date are scored against ground-truth outcomes.

Architecture and Interaction Tools

To facilitate long-horizon interaction, FutureSim provides agents with a minimal but powerful interface. Agents are placed in a sandbox using tools like bwrap to prevent leakage of information from the live internet or future files.

Agents interact with the environment through a set of core actions:

submit_forecast(question_id, outcomes): The agent registers or updates a probability distribution over possible outcomes for a specific question.
next_day(): This advances the simulation clock by 24 hours, triggering any scheduled event resolutions and updating the available news corpus.

Beyond these core actions, agents are equipped with a suite of tools to aid their reasoning. These include a shell for file manipulation and a hybrid semantic-keyword search tool. This search tool allows agents to query the news corpus, which contains millions of articles. Critically, the search_news tool is date-restricted; an agent simulating January 5th cannot "see" articles published on January 6th.

The environment's complexity is significant. For instance, a high-performing agent might consume over 12 million unique tokens and perform thousands of individual actions over the course of a single simulation run. This scale requires agents to implement context window management, such as summarizing past actions or using external memory files to retain key information across time steps.

Quantitative Metrics for Forecasting Accuracy

The evaluation of agents in FutureSim focuses on both accuracy and calibration. The researchers use two primary metrics: Top-1 Accuracy and the Brier Skill Score (BSS).

The Brier Skill Score is particularly important in forecasting because it rewards agents for being confident when they are right and penalizes them heavily for being confident when they are wrong. For a given question

A perfect score is 1, while a score of 0 is equivalent to abstaining or having no predictive power. A negative score indicates that the agent's predictions were worse than a uniform guess or abstention, often due to being confidently wrong.

Performance of Frontier Agents

The researchers tested several state-of-the-art models, including GPT 5.5, DeepSeek V4 Pro, and Claude Opus 4.6. The benchmark consisted of 330 questions curated from world news events resolving in early 2026—dates that fall after the training cutoffs for these models.

GPT 5.5 emerged as the highest-performing model, achieving a top-1 accuracy of 25% and a positive Brier Skill Score. However, a significant finding was that many open-weight models initially struggled, often producing negative BSS values. This suggests that while these models are capable of reasoning, their native "harnesses" (the way they are prompted and orchestrated) are not yet optimized for long-horizon forecasting and belief updating.

Behavioral analysis revealed several interesting trends:

Overconfidence: Even the best models exhibited overconfidence. For GPT 5.5, roughly 27.4% of its incorrect final predictions assigned a probability of at least
0.5
0.5 to the wrong answer.
Update Frequency: Models like DeepSeek V4 Pro were highly active, frequently updating their predictions as new information arrived. This activity generally led to better final accuracy compared to more conservative models.
Self-Conditioning: Agents occasionally fell into a trap of "self-conditioning," where they treated their own previous reasoning or memory entries as absolute truths, leading them to ignore newer, contradictory evidence.
The Role of Memory and Search

Ablation studies—experiments where specific features are removed to see their impact—highlighted the critical components of successful adaptation.

The researchers found that memory is indispensable. When agents were prevented from writing to or reading from a memory file, their performance dropped significantly. Memory serves as a repository for past search results, rationales, and lessons learned, helping to prevent "knowledge drift" as the simulation progresses over many days.

Agentic search also proved vital. Agents that were allowed to perform iterative, sequential searches were twice as accurate as those limited to a single search query. This underscores the importance of "uncertain search," where an agent uses the results of one query to refine its next question, gradually narrowing down the facts. Furthermore, the daily update of the news corpus was essential; agents provided with a static snapshot at the start of the simulation performed significantly worse than those who could search for new articles every simulated day.

The Anchoring

item.title: [论文] 一个中等模型，拿下了奥赛金牌
item.description:
你有没有想过，国际数学奥赛（IMO）或者物理奥赛（IPhO）这种级别的题，对AI来说到底难在哪儿？它们不是简单地套公式，而是要像人类选手一样，一步步推导、验证不同假设，最后构建出严谨又带点灵光的证明。过去要让AI达到奥赛金牌水平，路子通常很粗暴：要么把模型参数堆到天量，要么针对每个科目专门设计一套求解器。但这篇论文走了另一条路——用一个中等规模的统一模型，通过一套简单清晰的训练流程，就站上了金牌领奖台。

他们把这个模型家族叫做SU-01，核心架构是30B-A3B，也就是总参数量300亿，其中每次计算激活的只有30亿参数。相比动辄上千亿的大块头，这个尺寸显得相当“经济适用”。那它是怎么做到的呢？关键是一套三段式训练流水线：先监督微调（SFT）重塑行为习惯，再用两阶段强化学习把解题能力真正“刷”上去，最后在考试时允许模型多花点时间反复检查。

我们先看第一阶段，监督微调。这一步的目标不是灌知识，而是教模型学会一种“证明探路”的风格——它要学会自己尝试不同解法、自我检查、用奥赛裁判期待的那种严谨方式组织语言。这里面有个挺巧妙的设计：他们给训练数据排了个序，叫“反向困惑度”课程。困惑度（PPL）可以简单理解成模型对一段文字的“熟悉程度”，PPL越高说明越不熟悉。一般的课程学习喜欢从简单的、熟悉的例子开始，但这里反其道而行之：先把那些模型觉得最生疏、最困难的高PPL样本喂给它，然后再慢慢过渡到熟悉的低PPL样本。直觉上就像学冲浪，先让你去感受大浪，再回来练小浪，动作反而更能定型。实验显示，相比随机排序或者从低到高的传统课程，这种反向课程在AnswerBench这类基准上能带来明显提升。这个阶段用到了约33.8万个训练轨迹，里面有直接演示的正确解法，也有让模型自己先做一遍、再教它如何修正的“自我改进”数据。这样既保持了模型的一般科学常识，又帮它养成了严谨推导的习惯。

接下来是两个阶段的强化学习，这才是真正拉开差距的地方。第一阶段叫“粗粒度RL”，用的是可验证奖励——也就是只看答案对不对。模型做大量题，答对了就给正反馈，答错了就给负反馈。策略更新的算法叫GSPO，它会把同一道题的多次解答放在一起比较，看哪种推理路径更容易通向正确答案。这一步相当于给模型刷题强化解题效率。但光看答案对错是不够的，奥赛的证明题就算答案蒙对，逻辑过程可能漏洞百出。于是就进入了第二阶段“精粒度RL”：他们引入了一个专门评估证明过程的生成式奖励模型（数学上用的是DeepSeekMath-V2）。这个老师会通读整个推导链，给严谨性、完整性和条理打分。不仅如此，训练中还用了两种很实用的技巧：如果模型做错了，会让它先自我批评再尝试修正，这些修正路径也拿来训练，这叫“自我提纯”；而对于那些难得一见、做对了的超难题，会把成功的推理缓存下来，时不时回放一下，防止模型忘记怎么处理复杂逻辑（“经验回放”）。通过这两轮RL，模型就从一个会模仿答案的学生，变成了一个真正能严密推演的“专家”。

最后一部是“测试时扩展”。即便复习得再好，临场多思考一会儿总是有益的。他们就给模型在推理时加了额外的计算预算，允许它通过自我验证和迭代优化来回打磨答案。比如做一道难题，模型可以先给出一个解，然后自己检查一遍，发现不对就修正，再来一遍，直到满意为止。这个循环可以使推理链轻松突破10万tokens，相当于一篇小长篇小说的长度，但它一直保持逻辑连贯，最终交出严谨证明。

有了这套方法，SU-01在数学和物理奥赛的考题上实打实地达到了人类金牌得主水平，而且整个流程是开源的、可复现的。我觉得最吸引人的地方，是它证明了：不一定要把模型做得无比庞大，也不一定要给每个学科造单独的工具，统一、清晰的训练设计同样能通向顶尖推理。当然，这也不是全无短板。一方面，它在常规科学问答或需要广泛常识的任务上表现如何，论文没有展开讨论——可能还是一个“专才”；另一方面，测试时扩展虽然有效，但会大幅增加单次解题的推理成本，实际应用中需要权衡。而且，精密度RL的效果很依赖那个评估证明过程的奖励模型，如果奖励模型本身有偏见或不够强，就可能限制提升空间。

未来如果能把这种“专精化通才”的思路推广到更多领域，比如生物奥赛、编程竞赛，或者结合外部工具进行长链条研究，应该会很有意思。总之，看这篇论文让我觉得，搞定困难推理不一定非得拼蛮力，有时候一套精心设计的训练策略，就能让一个中等身材的模型迸发出专家级的光彩。

论文：Achieving Gold-Medal-Level Olympiad Reasoning via Simple and Unified Scaling [arXiv:2605.13301]

📄 论文原文概述
Olympiad-level competitions, such as the International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO) and the International Physics Olympiad (IPhO), represent some of the most rigorous benchmarks for human intelligence. These problems require multi-step logical deductions, the management of complex assumptions, and the construction of formal proofs that are both creative and technically precise. While artificial intelligence has made significant strides in general tasks, reaching the level of a gold-medalist in these specialized domains has traditionally required either massive model scaling or highly specialized, domain-specific solvers.

The SU-01 model family represents an effort to achieve this level of performance using a unified and efficient scaling recipe. Rather than building separate systems for math and physics, researchers developed a pipeline that transforms a post-trained reasoning backbone into a rigorous solver. This approach focuses on a "specializable-generalist" philosophy, where a single model (specifically a
30
B-A3B
30B-A3B architecture) is trained to excel in competition-style reasoning while maintaining the ability to transfer those skills across broader scientific fields.

Figure 1: The three-stage training and inference pipeline of SU-01, consisting of Supervised Fine-Tuning, two-stage Reinforcement Learning, and Test-Time Scaling.

The Unified Scaling Recipe

The core of this work is a three-stage process designed to instill and then scale rigorous reasoning behaviors. This process differs from current approaches that often rely on massive, closed-source datasets or opaque training routines. Instead, the recipe is modular:

Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): Reshapes the initial reasoning style toward disciplined, proof-oriented long-form output.
Two-Stage Reinforcement Learning (RL): Scales the behaviors acquired in SFT into expert problem-solving capabilities using both verifiable (answer-based) and generative (proof-based) rewards.
Test-Time Scaling (TTS): Allocates additional computational resources during inference to hard problems through self-verification and iterative refinement loops.

By following this sequence, the model transitions from a generalist that can solve simple problems to an expert capable of sustaining coherent reasoning trajectories exceeding
100
K
100K tokens.

Supervised Fine-Tuning and the Reverse-Perplexity Curriculum

The first stage of the pipeline focuses on behavior modification. The goal of SFT is to teach the model a specific "proof-search" style—encouraging it to explore different solution paths, check its own work, and present arguments with the rigor expected in an Olympiad.

A key challenge in fine-tuning a pre-existing reasoning model is preventing "catastrophic forgetting," where the model gains a new skill but loses its general scientific knowledge. To mitigate this, the researchers employed a Reverse-Perplexity Curriculum. In this setup, training examples are ordered by their perplexity (
𝑃
𝑃
𝐿
PPL) relative to the initial model policy.

The training starts with "unfamiliar" examples—those with high
𝑃
𝑃
𝐿
PPL—and gradually moves toward "familiar" examples with low
𝑃
𝑃
𝐿
PPL. This curriculum allows the model to adapt its behavioral patterns to the most challenging and different examples first, which stabilizes the learning process. Empirical results show that this ordering significantly improves the model's performance on benchmarks like AnswerBench compared to random ordering or an ascending perplexity curriculum.

Figure 2: The composition of the 338K SFT trajectories, including math, STEM, code, and self-improvement data.

The data used in this stage is a mixture of direct generation (where the model is shown the correct path) and self-improvement data (where the model is shown how to verify and refine its own initial attempts). This prepares the model for the subsequent reinforcement learning and inference stages.

Scaling Capabilities via Two-Stage Reinforcement Learning

Once the model has learned the desired style, it enters a two-stage reinforcement learning phase. This phase is critical for moving beyond simple imitation of training data and instead developing robust problem-solving strategies through trial and error.

Coarse RL (Verifiable Rewards)

The first sub-stage, Coarse RL, utilizes Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). In this stage, the model attempts to solve a large pool of problems, and the reward is binary: it receives a positive signal if the final answer is correct and a zero or negative signal if it is incorrect. The model's policy is updated using Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO), which looks at groups of sampled solutions to determine which reasoning paths are most likely to lead to the correct answer. This stage effectively boosts the model's raw problem-solving power and search efficiency.

Refined RL (Generative Rewards)

The second sub-stage, Refined RL, addresses the limitation of binary rewards. In mathematical proofs, a correct final answer does not guarantee a logically sound argument. To instill actual rigor, the researchers introduced a generative reward model. For mathematics, a specialized model (DeepSeekMath-V2) was used to evaluate the entire reasoning path for validity, completeness, and rigor.

During this stage, two additional mechanisms are used:

Self-Refinement: If the model fails a problem, it is prompted to critique its own reasoning and attempt a repair. These refinement trajectories are then used to update the policy.
Experience Replay: Rare successful trajectories for extremely difficult problems are stored in a buffer and replayed during training to ensure the model doesn't "forget" the complex logic required for the hardest tasks.
Test-Time Scaling: Reasoning via Iteration

Even an expert model can benefit from more "thinking time." Test-Time Scaling (TTS) is a method to improve performance by increasing the computational budget during the inference phase. Instead of a single "one-shot" attempt, SU-01 uses a self-verification and refinement loop.

The loop follows a four-step process:

Solver: The model generates an initial solution.
Refinement: The model revises the solution to improve clarity and fill gaps.
Verification: The model acts as a "critic," inspecting the solution for errors and producing a "bug report."
Verdict: Based on the verification, the system decides whether to accept the solution, reject it, or try another refinement.

This process can be repeated multiple times, and independent runs can be launched in parallel. This iterative approach allows the model to sustain reasoning trajectories that are significantly longer than standard generations, often reaching over
100
K
100K tokens for the most complex USAMO (USA Mathematical Olympiad) problems.

Figure 3: Distribution of token lengths across different phases of the Test-Time Scaling loop, showing the model's ability to maintain long-horizon reasoning.

Performance and Results

The effectiveness of this unified recipe is demonstrated through the model's performance on official Olympiad problems and scientific benchmarks. SU-01 achieved scores that meet or exceed the gold-medal thresholds for the International Physics Olympiad (IPhO) 2024 and 2025, and the International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO) 2025.

Competition SU-01 (Direct) SU-01 (w/ TTS) Gold Medal Line
